title: Cat Abdominal Heaving: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat breathing with their stomach? Learn about abdominal heaving, why it is an emergency, and how to help your pet right now.
content_html:
What is Abdominal Heaving?

Abdominal heaving occurs when a cat uses their belly muscles to assist with the process of breathing. In a healthy cat, respiration is quiet and involves minimal movement of the chest. When you see the abdomen noticeably expanding and contracting with every breath, it indicates that your cat is struggling to move air into or out of their lungs, a condition known as dyspnea.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

Abdominal heaving is considered a critical medical emergency. Cats are masters at hiding discomfort, so by the time they show visible signs of labored breathing, their oxygen levels may be dangerously low. If this is accompanied by open-mouth breathing, a blue or purple tint to the gums, or an extended neck, you must transport your cat to the nearest emergency veterinary hospital immediately. Do not wait for a scheduled appointment.

Why a Video or Photo Helps Triage

If it is safe to do so without delaying your trip to the vet, capturing a 5 to 10-second video of the breathing pattern can be life-saving. Cats often experience a spike in adrenaline during the car ride which may temporarily mask the heavy breathing once they arrive at the clinic. Providing the veterinary team with a visual record of the heaving at home allows them to accurately assess the severity and begin the correct life-saving treatments faster.

faqs.question: What does labored breathing look like in cats?
faqs.answer: Labored breathing often looks like 'stomach breathing,' where the belly moves in and out deeply. It may also include panting, flared nostrils, or a hunched posture with the elbows turned outward.

faqs.question: Can stress cause abdominal heaving?
faqs.answer: While stress can increase respiration rate, true abdominal heaving usually indicates an underlying medical issue such as asthma, heart failure, or fluid in the chest that requires immediate medical attention.

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my cat to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the car cool and quiet. Minimize handling to reduce stress, as extra exertion can make it harder for your cat to breathe. If possible, call the emergency clinic while en route so they can prepare oxygen therapy for your arrival.
